actions.thought: I hold Q9s on the Button (SB) in a heads-up match. This is a strong hand relative to a random hand (top 20-30%), with good playability due to being suited and connected. Being in position post-flop is a major advantage. Folding is incorrect due to hand strength. Limping is passive and fails to build the pot or define ranges. A raise is the standard play to seize the initiative, build the pot, and potentially take down the blinds immediately. With 100BB stacks, a standard min-raise to 200 is efficient.

I am in the Big Blind with 83o, a very weak starting hand. The opponent raised to 200 from the Button. While I only need to call 100 more into a 300 pot, 83o realizes equity very poorly out of position and is easily dominated. There is no reason to defend such a weak range early in the tournament. I will fold and wait for better spots.

In a heads-up match, 6d 4d is a strong speculative hand on the button. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and leverage my positional advantage post-flop. Folding this hand would be too tight, and raising is generally more effective than limping in this spot.

I am in the Big Blind with QJo facing a 3x raise (to 300) from the Button/Small Blind. In heads-up play, QJo is a strong hand well above the median strength. I am getting 2:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot that will be 600 total), requiring roughly 33% equity to break even. QJo has approximately 50% equity against a standard wide button opening range. While being out of position suggests caution, the hand is too strong to fold. A 3-bet is possible but calling keeps the opponent's range wide and manages the pot size with a medium-strength broadway hand. Therefore, calling is the most standard and profitable play.

I hold Top Pair with a good kicker (Queens with Jack kicker) on a somewhat coordinated board (Q-9-8). The board texture connects well with the opponent's Button raising range (straight draws like J-T, T-7, pairs like 9x, 8x). As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) would deny the opponent the chance to bluff with missed hands or bet worse value hands that might fold to a lead. I intend to check-call if the opponent bets, as my hand is strong enough to continue but not invulnerable.

On a board of 9h-8s-Qd, my 6d 4d has very little direct equity. This board texture is highly coordinated and likely to have connected with a lot of hands in the Big Blind's range, such as 10-9, J-9, 9-8, or any Queen. While I was the pre-flop aggressor, continuation betting into a wet board where I have almost no showdown value or strong draws (only a backdoor flush and a weak gutshot possibility) is risky. Checking behind allows me to see a free turn card and keeps the pot manageable, given that I am unlikely to force a fold from better hands on this specific texture.

I hold Top Pair (Queens) with a Jack kicker on a board of 9h-8s-Qd-7d. The Turn card (7d) is very coordinated, completing J-T straights and bringing a diamond flush draw. However, my Jack blocker makes the straight less likely for the opponent. Since the opponent checked back the flop, their range is likely capped and consists of marginal made hands or give-ups. While a probe bet has merit to deny equity, checking is superior here for two reasons: 1) Pot Control: The board is dangerous, and I do not want to face a raise. 2) Bluff Inducement: The opponent may perceive the scare card as a good opportunity to bluff. I plan to call a reasonable bet.

The turn 7d is a very strong card for my range and specifically for my hand, as it provides me with both a flush draw and an open-ended straight draw (any 5 makes a straight). Given that my opponent has checked for the second time, it is unlikely they have a powerhouse like J-10 or a set, as they would typically want to protect against the many draws present on this board. I will bet 400 (two-thirds pot) as a semi-bluff. This allows me to take the pot down immediately if they are weak, while still having significant equity (approximately 12-15 outs) to improve to a winning hand on the river if called.

I hold Top Pair (Queens) with a Jack kicker. The opponent checked back the flop on a wet board (Q-9-8), which often indicates a marginal hand or a give-up rather than a monster, as sets or strong draws usually bet the flop for protection/value. The Turn (7) completes the obvious J-T straight draw. However, I hold a Jack blocker, reducing the combinations of J-T the opponent can have. The opponent's delayed c-bet of 400 into 600 could represent the straight, but it could also be a bluff utilizing the scare card, or a value bet from a worse Queen or two pair. Given I checked twice, my hand is under-represented. I need roughly 28% equity to call. Against a range containing bluffs, worse pairs, and some straights, my hand is too strong to fold, but raising isolates me against nuts. Calling is the correct play.

I hold Top Pair (Queens) with a good kicker on a board that is now paired (8s on Flop, 8h on River) and has a completed straight draw (J-T) from the Turn. The opponent checked back the flop but bet the Turn. The River 8 changes the texture significantly, making trips possible, though less likely given the flop check-back. Leading out (donk betting) is generally poor here as it folds out bluffs and isolates me against better hands (Trips, Straights, Boats). My hand has significant showdown value, but sits in the 'bluff-catcher' category relative to the nuts. Checking is the optimal line to induce bluffs from missed draws or to check-call a reasonable value bet from a worse Queen or missed draw. I will check to the aggressor.

The river 8h pairs the board, which is a significant card. My 6d 4d has no showdown value, as even a missed draw like KJ or AJ would beat me with a higher kicker using the board's pair of eights. However, Pro called a 400-chip bet on the turn, suggesting they have a piece of the board (like a Queen, Nine, or Seven) or a straight draw. While a large bluff could potentially force a fold from a weak Nine or Seven, the risk is high given that this is only the second hand of the tournament. The 8 appearing on the river also makes it more likely that Pro could have trips if they were defending with a hand like 8-7, 8-9, or T-8. I will check behind and concede the pot if I am beaten, preserving my stack for better opportunities.

I hold 5h 3c on the Button (SB) in a heads-up match. This is a 5-3 offsuit hand, which is statistically very weak and disconnected. While playing heads-up requires a wide opening range (often 80%+), 53o falls into the bottom percentage of hands that are generally negative EV to play. Even with the positional advantage post-flop, the hand has very poor playability and equity. Since it is early in the match (Hand 3), there is no need to force action with trash hands. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

In a heads-up situation from the button, 7d 5d is a strong speculative hand with good post-flop playability. Raising is the standard play here to take control of the pot and apply pressure to the big blind. A 3x open (300 chips) is a solid sizing to build the pot while maintaining a manageable risk-to-reward ratio.

I hold 8s 7s in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button. This is a suited connector with good playability and decent equity against a wide Heads-Up opening range. The pot odds are 3:1 (calling 200 to win 600), requiring roughly 25% equity. 87s has significantly more than 25% equity against a standard button opening range. While a 3-bet is an option to apply pressure, calling is the standard play to realize equity and keep the pot manageable while playing out of position post-flop.

I hold a flush draw (8s7s) on a Ks Qd 3s board. While I have good equity, this board texture (two broadway cards) heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range (AK, KQ, sets of K/Q). Donk betting here is generally inadvisable as I am out of position against a range advantage. The standard play is to check to the aggressor. If they bet, I will likely continue via call or check-raise depending on sizing, as my draw is strong.

On a board of Ks Qd 3s, I have no direct connection with my 7d 5d. While this texture often favors the pre-flop raiser, I have almost no equity and the board is quite draw-heavy. Checking back is a prudent, low-risk play to see a free turn card. It keeps the pot manageable and avoids a potential check-raise on a board where I have no hand and very few outs.

On the Turn, the 7c improves my hand to a pair of Sevens in addition to my flush draw (8s7s). The board is now Ks Qd 3s 7c. The opponent checked back the Flop, which often indicates a capped range (weak Kx, Qx, or underpairs) or a give-up with air, though they could be trapping. With 3rd pair and a flush draw, I have decent showdown value and significant equity if behind. Leading out (donk betting) might fold out their bluffs that I'm currently beating or isolate me against better hands like Kx/Qx. Checking is the most robust play here: it allows me to realize my equity for free if they check back again, or I can comfortably call a bet given my pair plus flush draw outs. If I check, I keep their range wide.

I've picked up bottom pair on the turn with the seven of diamonds. Since Pro has checked for the second time, my hand now has some showdown value. Checking back allows me to control the pot and see a free river card. There's little merit in betting here as I'm unlikely to get a better hand to fold, and I don't want to get check-raised off my equity.

I hold a pair of Sevens (8s7s) on a K-Q-3-7-T board. The flush draw missed. The action went check-check on both the Flop and Turn. The opponent's range likely consists of marginal made hands (weak Kx, Qx, or underpairs) or Ace-high types that gave up. Betting here is unlikely to get called by worse hands (like A-high or 44-66) often enough to be profitable, and I will likely only be called by better (Tx, Qx, Kx). By checking, I allow the opponent to check back with worse hands for me to win at showdown, or I can evaluate if they decide to bluff. Given the passive line, checking to realize showdown value is the best play.

I have bottom pair (sevens) on a board with three overcards (K, Q, T). Both players have checked the flop and the turn. Pro has now checked for the third time on the river. Given the passive nature of the hand so far, my pair of sevens has significant showdown value against missed draws or lower pocket pairs. There is little value in betting as I'm unlikely to be called by a worse hand, and I'm not likely to fold out a better hand with a single bet in this small pot. Checking back is the most reliable way to realize my equity and win the pot.

I hold 7d 2h on the Button (SB), which is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. While I have position post-flop in heads-up play, the hand has extremely poor playability and equity against any random hand. Although my opponent folded to a raise in Hand 1, a sample size of one fold is insufficient to justify opening with 72o as a pure bluff exploit. The standard and most +EV play here is to simply fold.
QTo is a very strong hand in heads-up play. I will raise to 300, which has been my consistent opening size, to build the pot and leverage my positional advantage post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 6d 4h, facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the Button. My opponent has consistently used this 3x sizing. To call, I need to put in 200 chips into a total pot of 600, requiring roughly 33% equity realization. While 64o has some raw equity against a wide range, it plays very poorly out of position. It is easily dominated, and realizing equity is difficult. Against a 3bb open, this hand falls below the standard defense threshold. Folding is the prudent long-term play to conserve chips.

I am on the Button (SB) in a heads-up match with 7h 8s, an off-suit connector. In heads-up play, position is crucial, and 87o is well within the playable range for the button (top 60-70% of hands). I have folded 2 of my last 3 buttons (Hand 3 and 5), so I need to open this hand to prevent my opponent from exploiting a tight folding frequency. A min-raise to 200 is a standard, low-risk way to assert dominance, steal the blind, or build a pot where I will have a positional advantage post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind holding 63 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Pro has opened with a standard raise to 200. Although I am getting 3:1 odds on a call, 63o is near the bottom of the range and plays poorly out of position. I will fold here to conserve chips and wait for a better hand to play.

I am on the button with Ace-Queen of spades, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. My goal is to build a pot and exert pressure. A standard 3x raise to 300 is consistent with my previous winning strategy and forces the opponent to either pay to see a flop or fold their equity.

I hold Ace-Queen offsuit (AQo) in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand in heads-up play, dominating much of the opponent's Button opening range. The opponent (Flash) has raised to 300 (3BB), a sizing they have used consistently. While a 3-bet for value is a viable option to build the pot and take initiative, calling is also excellent strategy. Calling keeps the opponent's range wide (including weaker Ax and Qx hands that I dominate), controls the pot size while I am out of position, and protects my calling range. Since AQo lacks flush connectivity compared to AQs, playing a slightly smaller pot and evaluating the flop texture is prudent. I will call the additional 200 chips to see the flop.

I hold Top Pair with a strong kicker (Ace-Queen) on an Ace-high board with a flush draw (8d Ad 2c). Standard play in the Big Blind after calling a pre-flop raise is to check to the aggressor. Checking allows the opponent to make a continuation bet with their bluffs, semi-bluffs (flush draws), or weaker value hands (worse Aces, pocket pairs). Leading out (donk betting) might cause them to fold worse hands that would otherwise put chips in the pot. I am well-positioned to call a bet or evaluate the turn if checked back.

I have top pair with a strong kicker on a board with an Ace and a flush draw. Since Pro checked, I should bet for value and to protect my hand against potential draws or weaker pairs that might call. A half-pot bet of 300 is a standard sizing here.

I hold Top Pair with a strong kicker (AQ) on an [8d Ad 2c] board. The opponent has made a standard continuation bet of 300 (half-pot). Raising here would likely fold out the opponent's bluffs and isolate me against a range consisting of strong Aces (AK, A8, A2) or sets, which limits my upside. By calling, I keep the opponent's range wide, allowing them to continue bluffing with air or semi-bluffing with flush draws. I also retain the ability to extract value from worse Aces (A3-AJ) on later streets. My Queen of diamonds also provides a blocker to the nut flush draw and a backdoor draw of my own. Calling is the most profitable line to protect my checking range and navigate the turn out of position.

I hold Top Pair with a strong kicker (AQ) on a board of [8d Ad 2c Jh]. The turn Jack is a somewhat connective card, completing a straight for QT and giving two pair to AJ, but my hand remains very strong against the opponent's wide Button range. Having called the flop c-bet, the standard line out of position is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get called by better hands or strong draws while folding out the bluffs I want to keep in the pot. Checking allows the opponent to fire a second barrel with semi-bluffs (diamond draws, straight draws like KT) or value bet worse Aces. I intend to call a reasonable bet or evaluate the river if it goes check-check.

I have top pair with the best possible kicker (TPTK) on a board that hasn't changed much on the turn. My opponent has checked again after calling my flop bet. I should continue betting for value to get paid by weaker Aces and to charge any diamond flush draws that might be staying in. A bet of 800 into the 1200 pot represents roughly two-thirds of the pot, which is a standard value-heavy sizing here.

I hold Top Pair with a strong kicker (AQ) on a turn board of [8d Ad 2c Jh]. The opponent has fired a second barrel of 800 into a pot of 1200 (2/3 pot). This sizing is polarizing, representing either strong value (AJ, A8, Sets) or semi-bluffs (diamond draws, straight draws like QT/KT). While the Jack improves AJ to two pair and QT to a straight, my hand remains ahead of many value hands (AT, A9, weaker Ax) and all semi-bluffs. Raising would isolate me against hands that beat me, so calling is the optimal play to keep the opponent's bluffing range wide and realize my equity. I will call to see the river.

The river card is the 4h, making the final board 8d Ad 2c Jh 4h. The flush draw from the flop (diamonds) has missed, and the 4h is effectively a brick that changes very little. I hold Top Pair with a strong kicker (AQ). 

My opponent has shown aggression by betting the flop and the turn. Their range is polarized between strong value hands (AJ, Sets, QT straight) and missed draws (diamonds, KT, etc.). 

By checking, I accomplish two things:
1. I induce bluffs from the missed draws. If I bet, these hands will simply fold, and I gain nothing.
2. I allow the opponent to check back with worse showdown value (like AT or A9) that might not call a bet but would be happy to get to showdown.
3. I control the size of the pot against better hands. 

Leading out would likely only get action from hands that beat me (AJ, Sets) while folding out the hands I beat. Therefore, checking is the optimal line to maximize value from bluffs and minimize losses against the nuts.

I have top pair with a strong kicker (AQ) on a board that didn't see the diamond flush complete. Pro has check-called every street, which typically indicates a marginal made hand like a weaker Ace or a Jack. Since the river 4h is a blank, I should continue betting for value. A bet of 1400 (half the pot) is designed to get called by those weaker Aces while not over-committing if the opponent happens to have a slow-played monster like AJ or A8.

I hold Top Pair with a strong kicker (AQ) on a final board of [8d Ad 2c Jh 4h]. The opponent has fired a third barrel of 1400 into a pot of 2800. The river 4h is a blank that completes no draws, meaning the diamond flush draws and straight draws from the flop and turn have missed. The opponent's range is polarized between strong value hands (AJ, A8, AK, Sets) and missed draws/bluffs. The bet size of half-pot gives me 3:1 odds, requiring me to be good only 25% of the time. Since I am at the very top of my check-calling range (blocking some value like AQ/Qd draws but unblocking other bluffs), folding here would be too exploitable. I must call to catch bluffs and split with other AQ hands.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with 4d 8h, which is an extremely weak hand (bottom 10% of hands). While Button ranges in Heads-Up play are wide, 84o is usually outside of a profitable opening range, especially given that it is unsuited and unconnected. My previous play has shown I fold the bottom of my range (53o, 72o) and raise playable hands. Folding here is the standard and prudent play to preserve chips.

I am on the button in a heads-up match, which gives me the advantage of acting last on all post-flop streets. While Queen-Three offsuit is a relatively weak hand, standard heads-up strategy involves opening a wide range of hands from the small blind to capitalize on position. I have consistently been raising to 300 in previous hands when I decide to play, and I will maintain that sizing here to keep my range unprotected and exert pressure on the big blind.

The opponent has raised to 3BB (300 chips) from the Button. I am in the Big Blind with T7o. While I get 3:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win 600), T7o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Against a 3x open, I should be defending a tighter range than against a min-raise. T7o is likely dominated by the opponent's value range and will struggle to realize its equity post-flop. Since I am currently leading in chip count (10550 vs 9050), I can afford to fold this marginal hand and wait for better opportunities or a cheaper price to defend.

I hold Pocket Queens (Qh Qc), a premium starting hand, on the Button (SB). In Heads-Up play, I will act last post-flop, giving me a significant positional advantage. Looking at my history, I have opened to 200 with weaker hands (Q9s in Hand #1, 78o in Hand #7). Therefore, a standard min-raise to 200 is the best play to build the pot for value while disguising the strength of my hand by balancing my opening range. This size also leaves room for the aggressive opponent (Flash) to 3-bet, which I would happily call or 4-bet.

I am in the big blind with Ten-Six offsuit. The button has raised to 200, making it 100 more for me to call into a pot that will be 400. At 4:1 pot odds, T6o has sufficient equity to defend against a standard button opening range in a heads-up match. I will call to see a flop and play the hand out from there.

I defended the big blind with T6o. The flop comes A-8-2 rainbow with two hearts. This board heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range as it includes many Ace-x combinations. I have missed the flop entirely with no pairs or meaningful draws. I will check and look to fold if the opponent bets, as I have very little equity to continue in this pot.

I hold Pocket Queens (Qh Qc) on an A-high board (2d 8h Ah). As the pre-flop raiser, the Ace hits my range well, but it also hits the Big Blind's calling range. This is a classic 'way ahead / way behind' scenario. If the opponent has an Ace, I am drawing thin and want to keep the pot small. If the opponent has a smaller pair or air, I want to induce bluffs or get value on later streets. Betting here often folds out hands I beat (King-high, Jack-high) and isolates me against hands that beat me (Ax) or strong draws. Checking back balances my checking range (protecting it so I don't only check when weak) and allows me to bluff-catch on the turn or river. I have the Queen of Hearts, which blocks some flush draws and gives me backdoor equity. Therefore, checking is the most prudent play to control the pot size and realize my showdown value.

After defending my big blind with T6o, the flop was checked through on an Ace-high board. The 5s on the turn doesn't improve my hand. Since Pro checked back on the flop, they likely don't have a strong Ace, but I still have zero showdown value with Ten-high. I will check to see if I can get to a cheap showdown or if Pro will continue to check down. I'm prepared to fold to most bets since I have no equity.

I checked back the flop with Pocket Queens on the Ace-high board (Ah 8h 2d) to control the size of the pot, as this is a classic 'way ahead / way behind' scenario. The turn brings the 5s, which is largely a blank. Flash checks to me again. Betting here has limited utility: I likely only get called by an Ace (which beats me) or a strong draw, while folding out the hands I beat (like middle pairs or air). By checking back, I deny check-raise opportunities, keep the opponent's range wide, and potentially induce bluffs on the river. I have Showdown Value that I want to realize as cheaply as possible. My Queen of Hearts also provides some protection/blockers against flush draws.

The board has run out A-8-2-5-K. I have Ten-high, which has virtually no showdown value. However, the action has been checked through on both the flop and the turn, suggesting that my opponent likely does not have a strong hand like an Ace. The King on the river is a scare card, but since I have checked every street, leading out now would look polarized and might not be believable. I will check and hope to get to a cheap showdown, though I expect to lose to most of my opponent's range if they check back, and I will fold if they bet.

I hold Pocket Queens on a board of Ah 8h 2d 5s Kd. The river King is an unfortunate card, downgrading my hand to third pair. Flash has checked to me on all three streets (Flop, Turn, River). This line suggests he either has a weak made hand (like an 8, 5, or 2), a missed draw (hearts), or he was trapping/pot-controlling a weak Ace. The arrival of the King hits a portion of his range that might have been floating or checking down (Kx). If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by worse hands (8x will likely fold), and I will certainly be called by better hands (Ax, Kx). Therefore, there is no value in betting. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value against his missed draws and lower pairs without risking more chips in a spot where I am often beat by top or second pair.

K4o is a standard opening hand from the button in heads-up play. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to take control of the pot and potentially win it pre-flop, consistent with my earlier betting patterns.

I hold Ts 2s in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the opponent on the Button. The opponent has consistently opened for 3x (300) rather than a min-raise (200). This larger sizing worsens my pot odds (requiring 33% equity to call rather than 25%). T2s is a very weak hand, sitting at the bottom of my range. Although it is suited, it is easily dominated and plays poorly out of position. I have folded better hands like T7o and 64o to this same sizing previously. Without a specific read that the opponent folds excessively post-flop, defending T2s against a 3bb open is -EV. I will fold.

I hold K9s on the button, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play (top 20-30% of hands). My opponent, Flash, has folded to 2 out of 3 of my previous button raises, indicating he might be playing fit-or-fold out of the big blind. Raising allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot immediately, or play a pot in position with a hand that has good flush and top-pair potential. A standard min-raise to 200 is consistent with my previous play and offers good risk/reward odds.

I am in the Big Blind with 10-2 offsuit. Pro has opened for a minimum raise to 200. While I am getting 3-to-1 odds to call (100 more to win a total pot of 400), T2o is one of the weakest hands in poker and performs very poorly out of position. Given that we are still early in the 150-hand session and stacks are deep, I prefer to fold this hand rather than leak chips with a low-equity hand that is difficult to play post-flop.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with Ace-Ten offsuit (Ah Td). This is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, well above the average range. The pot currently consists of my 50 small blind and Pro's 100 big blind. I have 9,200 chips remaining. I want to raise for value and to take control of the hand. Standard open sizing in this tournament has been around 3x. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and apply pressure on the Big Blind.

The opponent (Flash) has been opening to 300 frequently from the Button/SB, indicating a wide range. I am in the Big Blind with Q6s, needing to call 200 into a pot of 400 (getting 2:1 odds). Q6s has sufficient equity (roughly 45-50% against a wide opening range) to justify a call. Being out of position post-flop, calling is standard to keep the pot manageable while realizing equity. Folding is too tight given the hand strength and pot odds.

I am out of position on the flop with Queen-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9 to make a 6-high straight). The board (Th 8s 7s) is very coordinated and wet, likely hitting the opponent's raising range or at least offering many draws. Standard play OOP is to check to the pre-flop raiser. Donk betting here accomplishes little as better hands call and worse hands fold. I will check and evaluate based on the opponent's action.

I have top pair with top kicker (AT on a T-8-7 rainbow board). This is a very strong hand in heads-up play, although the board is somewhat coordinated with straight and flush draws. Pro has checked to me on the flop. I should bet here to extract value from weaker tens, eights, sevens, and various draws (like straight draws with a 9 or 6, or spade flush draws). A bet of around half the pot is standard to build the pot and charge drawing hands. I will bet 300 into the 600 pot.

I defended the Big Blind with Q6s against a 3x open. The flop comes Th 8s 7s, which is a very wet and coordinated board that likely connects well with the opponent's opening range (containing many Tx, 9x, Jx, and flush draws). I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9) and one overcard (Queen). However, my gutshot draws to the low end of the straight (6-7-8-9-T), which is dangerous, and I have no flush draw potential with diamonds. Facing a half-pot c-bet (300 into 600), my pot odds are 25%, but realizing this equity out of position on such a dynamic board is difficult. My Queen-high has little showdown value, and my outs are not 'clean'. Folding is the disciplined decision here to preserve chips for better spots.

I hold Kc7c on the Button (Small Blind). In heads-up play, K7s is a strong starting hand (top ~33%) well worth playing. My opponent 'Flash' has folded to my min-raises in the Big Blind several times (Hands 1, 7, 13). Raising to 200 is the standard aggressive play to steal the blinds or build a pot in position with a playable hand. Limping is passive and allows the BB to realize equity for free. Therefore, I will make a standard min-raise.

93o is a very weak starting hand that plays poorly out of position. Even though I am getting a good price to call (100 chips to win a 300 chip pot), the lack of connectivity and high-card value makes it a losing play in the long run against a professional opponent. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 8d 7h, a standard hand to open in heads-up play. I have been consistently opening to 300 from the button in previous hands to exert pressure and take advantage of my positional advantage. 87 offsuit is well within the range of hands to raise for value and to maintain an aggressive image.

I hold KQo in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand in a heads-up match, significantly ahead of the opponent's wide button opening range. The opponent has raised to 300 (3BB). While a 3-bet is a valid option to take the initiative, I am out of position and my previous play history (specifically Hand #8 with AQo) suggests a pattern of defending by calling against this opponent's 3x opens. Calling keeps the opponent's range wide, allowing me to dominate weaker Kings and Queens, and keeps the pot size controlled while playing out of position. The price is good (200 more to win a pot of 600), so I will call.

I hold Top Pair with a strong kicker (KQ) on a dry, rainbow board (K-7-2). Being out of position against an aggressive opponent who has shown a tendency to c-bet (e.g., Hand #8, Hand #14), checking is the standard and most profitable line. It allows the opponent to bluff with their wide button range or bet with a worse King. There are no significant draws to protect against, so there is no urgency to bet. I will check and evaluate their action, planning to call or check-raise if they bet.

I am in position on a K-7-2 rainbow board with middle pair (7s). This is a very dry board where my opponent, Pro, will likely check-fold many of their weaker hands. Since Pro checked to me, I have the opportunity to bet for value and protection. A bet of around half the pot is appropriate here to charge draws or weaker pairs (like 2x or small pocket pairs) and to potentially take the pot down immediately if the opponent has nothing. I hold a King-high straight (2-3-4-5-6) on a board of 6s-4c-2d-3c-Ts. The only hand that beats me is 7-5 (making a 3-7 straight), which is a small portion of the opponent's range. I hold Kd8d on a very coordinated 6d-7h-5h board. This gives me an Open-Ended Straight Draw (needing a 4 or 9), a backdoor flush draw, and a King overcard. 3. Showdown: I have showdown value beating A-high, K-high, and missed straight draws (like J9/KJ). Checking back allows me to win against these hands without risking chips against a random pair.

The most profitable play is to check behind and take the showdown.
